contexts.text: What we have done above can be interpreted in terms of  a two-person zero-sum game in which  are Nash equilibrium objects.

From the above definition, it is clear that the value  has two alternative interpretations: * by playing the appropriate mixed strategy, the maximizing player can assure himself at least (no matter what the column player chooses) * by playing the appropriate mixed strategy, the minimizing player can make sure that the maximizing player will not get more than  (irrespective of what is the maximizing player’s choice) A famous theorem of Nash (1951) tells us that there always exists a mixed strategy Nash equilibrium for any *finite* two-player zero-sum game.
Moreover, von Neumann’s Minmax Theorem {cite}`neumann1928theorie` implies that Nash equilibria of a finite two-player zero-sum game solve a linear programming problem.
